title: Muhammara
subtitle: Syrian roasted red pepper and walnut dip
excerpt: Roasted red peppers blended with walnuts, pomegranate molasses, cumin, and chilli into a deep-flavoured dip that is thick, slightly sweet, earthy, and warming. A Syrian speciality, related to but distinct from hummus — richer and more complex, best eaten with pitta and raw vegetables or as a sauce alongside grilled meat.
sourceNotes: Muhammara is a Syrian dip, particularly associated with Aleppo, where Aleppo pepper is the traditional chilli. Claudia Roden documents it in 'The New Book of Middle Eastern Food' (2000). The pomegranate molasses is the ingredient that distinguishes muhammara from a simple red pepper and walnut dip, adding a sharp fruity sourness that balances the richness of the nuts.

recipe.batchNotes: Make a large batch — muhammara keeps for 5 days in the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 3 days ahead. The flavour improves after a few hours as the spices mellow.

content.text: The red peppers need to be thoroughly charred on the outside — whether roasted in a hot oven, held over a gas flame, or placed under a grill until black. The char softens the flesh and adds a faint smokiness. After charring, the peppers go into a covered bowl where the steam loosens the skins so they peel off easily. Only then are the peppers ready to process.

The walnuts should be lightly toasted before processing — a dry pan over medium heat for 4 to 5 minutes brings out the oil and deepens their flavour. The final dip should be thick enough to hold its shape on a spoon, with visible texture from the walnuts. Finish with a generous drizzle of good olive oil and eat with pitta or flatbread.

Place the {{pepper-red}} directly on the rack of a 220°C fan oven, or hold over a gas flame, turning occasionally, until the skin is black and blistered all over. Transfer to a bowl, cover tightly with cling film, and leave for 15 minutes. Peel off the skins, remove the seeds, and discard.

Place the peeled peppers, {{walnuts}}, {{pomegranate-molasses}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{chilli-flakes}}, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, {{lemon}} juice, {{garlic}}, and salt in a food processor. Pulse to a coarse, textured paste — not completely smooth. Taste and adjust: more pomegranate molasses for sweetness, more lemon for sharpness, more chilli for heat. Transfer to a dish, drizzle with olive oil, and serve with pitta.

items.symptom: Dip is too thin and loose
items.cause: Peppers retained too much moisture or walnuts not toasted
items.fix: Pat the peeled peppers dry before processing. Toasted walnuts release less moisture than raw ones and give the dip more body

items.cause: Not enough pomegranate molasses or walnuts not toasted
items.fix: Pomegranate molasses is the backbone — taste before and after adding it. Toasted walnuts add significant depth; raw walnuts are bitter and flat

items.cause: Walnuts over-toasted or walnut skins very tannic
items.fix: Toast walnuts lightly — 4 to 5 minutes in a dry pan until fragrant, not dark. If bitter after processing, add more pomegranate molasses and a little honey to balance

items.cause: Not steamed long enough after charring
items.fix: Cover the hot peppers tightly for at least 15 minutes. The trapped steam loosens the skin. If still difficult, try peeling under cold running water

content.text: Muhammara comes from Aleppo, Syria's second city and historically one of the great culinary centres of the Arab world. It is typically made with Aleppo pepper (also called pul biber), a mild, fruity dried chilli with a faint oiliness, which is available in specialty and Middle Eastern shops. Standard chilli flakes are a reasonable substitute. Muhammara appears on mezze tables alongside hummus and mutabal as a dip, and doubles as a sauce for grilled meats and fish. The combination of pomegranate molasses and walnuts appears in several Syrian and Levantine dishes and reflects the region's skill in building complex, layered flavours from a small number of good ingredients.
